channel.title: Visual Studio Code on Tue Nguyen
channel.description: Recent content in Visual Studio Code on Tue Nguyen

item.title: Code formatting
item.description: Code formatting (or code refactoring) refers to ways of organizing and structuring your code. It is an essential aspect of software development with the ultimate goal to make yor codebase more consistent, readable, and maintainable. Prettier is an excellent extension that makes code formatting in VS Code become much simpler. Follow the steps below to install and config Prettier. Installation Go to Extensions (Ctrl + Shift + X), search for Prettier - Code formatter, and install it.

item.title: Cool features
item.description: Zen mode Zen mode is distract-free mode that hides away unnecessary detail and allows you focus just on the code. The zen mode can be toggled using Ctrl + K Z. You can also quickly exit the zen mode by double pressing Esc. This is the layout of the normal mode. And this is the layout of the zen mode. Minimap For a large file with many lines of code, you can quickly scroll up and down the file using the minimap on the right side of the editor.

item.title: Interface
item.description: The default layout of VS Code is divided into 6 main parts as follows. Menu Bar Activity Bar Side Bar Editor Panel Status Bar Menu Bar This is the narrow bar at the very top. It provides a GUI for essential operations grouped into different menus such as File, Edit, View, etc. However, most of those operations can be done very quickly with hotkeys. Activity Bar This is the narrow bar at the leftmost.

item.title: Introduction
item.description: What is VS Code? Visual Studio Code (or VS Code) is a free, open-source, lightweight, and cross-platform code editor developed by Microsoft based on the Electron Framework. It is one of the most popular code editors used and recommended by programmers at all levels, from beginners to experienced professionals. Features Here are some of the highlighted features that have made VS Code popular. Free. You pay nothing to use it. Lightweight.

item.title: Keyboard shortcuts
item.description: This post summarizes some of the most common keyboard shortcuts that can significantly boost your productivity while working with VS Code. In case you forget or want to search for a particular hotkey, you can press Ctrl + K Ctrl + S. It will show the key binding table together with a search box for you to type in the keywords. Navigation Go to settings: Ctrl + , Go to explorer: Ctrl + Shift + E Go to version control: Ctrl + Shift + G Go to debugger: Ctrl + Shift + D Go to find and replace (global): Ctrl + Shift + F Go to extensions: Ctrl + Shift + X Search Search for file by name: Ctrl + P Search for text globally: Ctrl + Shift + P Search for text in the current file: Ctrl + F Go to line number: Ctrl + G Search for symbol: Ctrl + Shift + O Command palette Go to command palette: Ctrl + Shift + P Creation New VS Code instance: Ctrl + Shift + N New unnamed file: Ctrl + N New file at specific subfolder: Ctrl + Alt + N (need Advanced New File extension) Terminals Toggle terminal: Ctrl + ~ Editor Operations on tabs or groups Switch between tabs: Ctrl + Tab (forward) or Ctrl + Shift + Tab (backward) Close current tab: Ctrl + W Close all tabs in current group: Ctrl + K W Close all tabs: Ctrl K Ctrl W Go to leftmost editor group: Ctrl + 1 Go to center editor group: Ctrl + 2 Go to rightmost editor group: Ctrl + 3 Operations on texts Copy current line: Ctrl + C Select current word: Ctrl + D Move current line: Alt + Up/Down Duplicate current line: Shift + Alt Up/Down Expand selection: Shift + Alt + Right Shrink selection: Shift + Alt + Left Modes Toggle zen mode: Ctrl + K Z (or double Esc to exit)

item.title: Markdown
item.description: Default support VS Code supports Markdown natively, meaning that it recognizes any file with .md extensions as a Markdown file and provides various supports such as syntax highlighting, outline, snippets, etc. Smart text editing VS Code supports many hotkeys that mimic those that you normally find when working with a rich text editor like Microsoft Word. Here are some examples. Make bold with Ctrl + B Make italic with Ctrl + I Auto extend list items, block quotes, when hitting Enter Auto extend blockquote when hitting Enter It also provides some convenient hotkeys.

item.title: Personalization
item.description: This is my preferred settings for VS Code. Editor Editor font size: 18 px Word wrap: on Word wrap column: 80 characters Theme Best choice: One Dark Pro Flat Next best choices: One Dark Pro, Visual Studio Dark Terminal To be updated

item.title: Settings
item.description: Settings allow you to customize VS Code in various aspects so that you feel most comfortable and productive. User and workspace settings There are two types of settings: user and workspace. User settings apply globally for every project whenever you open VS Code. Workspace settings only apply to the project that is currently open. Open settings Settings can be accessed and modified either through GUI or through the JSON file. I prefer the latter although it seems intimidating at first.

item.title: Themes
item.description: You will spend a lot of time looking at the IDE while writing code. Thus, it is crucial to have a theme that is nice to the eyes. Fortunately, there are a lot of beautiful themes available on VS Code marketplace, and it is very easy to install and activate a new theme. Types of themes In VS Code, themes are categorized into 3 types as follows. Color themes. A color theme change both the GUI icons (e.
